overview: This week I built a conversational Feature Intake Copilot that replaces traditional feature request forms with an adaptive AI-driven conversation, transforming messy stakeholder input into structured specs ready for PM review. The system uses a two-gate architecture — Claude handles the conversational extraction, and the PM makes the final quality call — achieving 94% ground truth extraction accuracy across three test personas. I also resolved a persistent tool-call bug by splitting each turn into two separate API calls, one for language and one for structured extraction.

keyPoints.title: 94% ground truth extraction accuracy across three stakeholder personas
keyPoints.description: The copilot captured an average of 5.7 out of 6 key fields compared to a hand-written skilled-PM baseline, with perfect scores for the marketing manager and engineering lead scenarios.

keyPoints.title: Adaptive questioning strategy reads signal density, not persona labels
keyPoints.description: Rather than branching on predefined personas, the copilot adjusts its question strategy based on what's present or missing — vague requests get extraction questions, technically detailed ones get reverse extraction for business justification.

keyPoints.title: Two-gate architecture separates stakeholder experience from PM quality decision
keyPoints.description: The copilot's gate never blocks submission and treats 'I don't know' as signal, while the PM's gate is where the structured accept/reject quality call is made with full rationale and override capability.

keyPoints.title: A full day of debugging resolved by splitting each turn into two API calls
keyPoints.description: When both a natural language response and a tool call were available in the same API call, the model consistently treated the tool call as the primary action and stubbed out the conversational text, requiring a two-call split per turn to fix.
keyPoints.sectionName: The Two-Call Split: Separate What the Model Says from What It Knows

keyPoints.title: Claude misclassified feature type in 1 of 3 test cases (67% accuracy)
keyPoints.description: The sales engineer scenario produced an incorrect feature type classification, reinforcing that PM override of the copilot's pre-selected classification remains a necessary part of the review workflow.
keyPoints.sectionName: Claude is Surprisingly Bad at Feature Type Classification

keyPoints.title: Sonnet-to-Opus advisor tool reintroduced for ambiguous intake decisions
keyPoints.description: Unlike last week where the advisor added no value for structured rubric scoring, I brought it back here because intake classification and borderline recommendations are genuinely ambiguous — though I'm still uncertain whether the cost is justified.

keyPoints.title: Conversations accommodate incomplete knowledge where forms punish it
keyPoints.description: The copilot's ability to say 'the product team can figure that part out' and move forward is a fundamental design advantage over forms, which block progress when a stakeholder can't answer a required field.
keyPoints.sectionName: Pipelines and Conversations Are Fundamentally Different Products
